title: Shipfix-Global Market Update
summary:
Main
Weaker demand across the dry bulk segments contributed to the Baltic Dry Index falling by more than four per cent on Tuesday. In contrast, many commodities advanced, supported by a weaker dollar and supply concerns amid rising geopolitical tensions. By Ulf Bergman

Macro/Geopolitics
Yesterday, the International Monetary Fund revised its outlook for global growth this year upwards.

children.title: Main
children.summary: Weaker demand across the dry bulk segments contributed to the Baltic Dry Index falling by more than four per cent on Tuesday. In contrast, many commodities advanced, supported by a weaker dollar and supply concerns amid rising geopolitical tensions.

children.title: Macro/Geopolitics
children.summary: Yesterday, the International Monetary Fund revised its outlook for global growth this year upwards. Amid a more positive view of the US economy and the extensive support that Beijing provides to the Chinese economy, the IMF expects that the global economy will expand by 3.1 per cent in 2024.

children.title: Commodity Markets
children.summary: After beginning the new week in the red, crude oil prices bounced back during yesterday's session. Renewed concerns that the mounting tensions in the Middle East would affect supplies contributed to the April Brent futures rising by 0.8 per cent during yesterday's trading, ending the session at 82.50 dollars per barrel.

children.title: Freight and Bunker Markets
children.summary: Tuesday saw all of the Baltic Exchange's dry bulk indices in the red. The headline Baltic Dry Index retreated by 4.3 per cent, with much of the downward momentum originating from the capesizes.

children.title: The View from the Shipfix Desk
section_path: The View from the Shipfix Desk
children.section_path_text: The View from the Shipfix Desk
children.summary: According to data released yesterday, the Euro Area avoided a recession during last year's final quarter by the smallest of margins. The zone's economy expanded by 0.1 per cent during the three months compared to the same period in 2022.
